note_data.title: Coordination and response
keyPoints: The **nervous system** consists of the central nervous system (CNS) and peripheral nervous system for rapid coordination
**Hormones** are chemical substances produced by endocrine glands and transported in blood plasma to target organs
A **reflex action** is a rapid, automatic response to a stimulus that does not involve conscious thought
**Synapses** are junctions between neurons where electrical impulses are transmitted by chemical neurotransmitters
The **brain** controls and coordinates body functions including conscious actions and memory
**Insulin** is a hormone produced by the pancreas that reduces blood glucose concentration by promoting glucose uptake by cells
**Glucagon** is produced by the pancreas and increases blood glucose concentration by stimulating glucose release from the liver
**Homeostasis** is the maintenance of constant internal conditions in the body
Sense organs contain **receptors** that detect stimuli and convert them into electrical impulses

definitions.definition: A chemical substance produced by an endocrine gland and transported by blood to a target organ where it produces a specific effect
A rapid, automatic response to a stimulus that does not involve conscious thought
A junction between two neurons where electrical impulses are transmitted by chemicals called neurotransmitters
A hormone produced by the pancreas that reduces blood glucose concentration
The maintenance of constant internal conditions

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Give detailed account of process in correct sequence
State similarities AND differences between nervous and hormonal control

modelAnswer.question: Explain how the reflex arc works when a person touches a hot object. (4 marks)
modelAnswer.answer: Receptor in skin detects heat stimulus and generates electrical impulse. Sensory neuron carries impulse to spinal cord. Relay neuron in spinal cord passes impulse to motor neuron. Motor neuron carries impulse to effector muscle which contracts to withdraw hand.
markBreakdown: Mark 2: Sensory neuron carries impulse to CNS
Mark 3: Relay neuron connects sensory to motor neuron
Mark 4: Motor neuron carries impulse to effector/muscle contracts

commonMistakes: Never say 'hormones travel through the nervous system' — always say 'hormones are transported in blood'
Students often confuse insulin and glucagon effects — insulin DECREASES glucose, glucagon INCREASES glucose
Never say 'brain controls reflexes' — reflex actions bypass the brain via spinal cord
Students write 'nerve' instead of 'neuron' — use neuron for individual cells
Never say 'impulses cross synapses' — impulses are converted to chemical neurotransmitters at synapses
examinerTips: Reflex arc diagrams appear almost every year - practice drawing with correct labels
Always mention 'does not involve conscious thought' when defining reflex actions
For hormone questions, always state: production site, transport method, target organ, and effect
Synapse questions often ask about one-way transmission - neurotransmitters only go from presynaptic to postsynaptic
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Reflex arc diagram showing: stimulus → receptor → sensory neuron → relay neuron (in spinal cord) → motor neuron → effector → response. Must include synapse positions and direction of impulse flow.
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q4 (6 marks) — they asked students to explain how the eye adjusts to bright and dim light conditions, including the role of the iris and pupil
This topic was tested in O/N 2022 P1 Q15 (1 mark) — multiple choice question on which part of the nervous system controls heart rate and breathing
This topic was tested in M/J 2022 P2 Q6(c) (4 marks) — students had to describe the pathway of a reflex action from stimulus to response, naming all structures involved
This topic was tested in O/N 2023 P2 Q3 (5 marks) — question required explanation of how auxins cause phototropism in plant shoots, including unequal distribution
This topic was tested in M/J 2021 P1 Q18 (1 mark) — asked which hormone increases blood glucose concentration when levels are too low

workedExample.question: Describe how a reflex action occurs when a person touches a hot object. Include the pathway taken by nerve impulses. (6 marks)
steps: Step 1: Identify the stimulus - hot object detected by temperature receptors in the skin
Step 2: Trace the pathway - nerve impulse travels along sensory neuron to the spinal cord
Step 3: Processing - impulse passes through relay neuron in spinal cord
Step 4: Response pathway - impulse travels along motor neuron to effector muscle
Step 5: Response - muscle contracts to withdraw hand from hot object
Step 6: Explain why reflex is important - automatic, fast response prevents tissue damage
workedExample.answer: Temperature receptors in the skin detect the hot object (stimulus). A nerve impulse travels along the sensory neuron to the spinal cord. In the spinal cord, the impulse passes through a relay neuron. The impulse then travels along a motor neuron to the muscle (effector). The muscle contracts, causing the hand to be withdrawn quickly. This reflex action is automatic and fast, preventing serious tissue damage from the heat.
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark each for: stimulus detection by receptors, sensory neuron to spinal cord, relay neuron in spinal cord, motor neuron to muscle, muscle contraction response, explanation of protective function

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused sensory and motor neurons in reflex arc diagrams' — always check that sensory neurons carry impulses TO the CNS and motor neurons carry impulses FROM the CNS

Examiner Report 2022: 'Students often wrote vague answers about 'nerves' instead of being specific' — always distinguish between central nervous system (brain and spinal cord) and peripheral nervous system (all other nerves)
Examiner Report 2023: 'Common error was stating that auxins are produced in roots for phototropism' — remember that auxins for shoot phototropism are produced in the shoot tip, not the roots
note_data.keyDiagram: Students must be able to draw and label a reflex arc diagram showing: receptor (in skin), sensory neuron with arrow pointing toward spinal cord, relay neuron in the spinal cord (gray matter), motor neuron with arrow pointing away from spinal cord toward effector muscle. Include labels for stimulus, spinal cord cross-section, and response. Arrows must show direction of nerve impulse flow. Some questions may also require a simple eye diagram showing cornea, lens, iris, pupil, retina, and optic nerve.
note_data.quickRecap: Coordination involves detecting stimuli and producing responses. The nervous system uses electrical impulses for fast responses like reflexes, while the endocrine system uses hormones for slower, longer-lasting effects. Key reflex pathway: receptor → sensory neuron → relay neuron → motor neuron → effector. Plants respond to stimuli using growth substances like auxins, causing tropisms such as shoots growing toward light (phototropism).
